summary: Patrick McKenzie reports progress on his second software project, Kalzumeus, built with Ruby on Rails. He estimates the project at roughly 15 classes, similar in complexity to Bingo Card Creator. He discusses his IDE choice (Netbeans), the tradeoffs between Ruby and Java development workflows, and his plan to save money by using a free open-source web design template instead of hiring a designer. He also briefly entertains the idea of going full-time on his software business but decides it's too risky for now.
key_points: Kalzumeus is comparable in complexity to Bingo Card Creator (~15 classes) but Rails development trades boilerplate coding time for debugging typos and misnamed variables
He chose Netbeans over Eclipse for Rails development and created custom macros to reduce Rails verbosity
He plans to save hundreds of dollars by using a free OSWD template instead of hiring a UI designer, with the option to upgrade later if the product succeeds
He calculates he could survive about a year going full-time on his software business but considers it too risky as a primary plan
He draws an insightful comparison between Java/Ruby (optimizing for maintenance programmer comprehension) versus Perl (optimizing for creation programmer keystroke efficiency)
money_quotes: Much more code is actually sticking to the screen and its been a matter of hours since I did a rm -rf * on the project directory.
I've noticed so far that I'm spending much, much less time on nuts&bolts programming than I typically do in Java, and much, much more time on chasing down misnamed variables, typos, and the like.
I do generally like the idea of prioritizing maintenance programmer brainsweat over creation programmer finger time.
There will probably be exactly one class which will require anything close to cleverness while coding.
I actually have a perverse affection for concurrency issues. They're one of the only places where I'm a halfway decent programmer.
I also have found a way to save myself a few hundred dollars for launch: cut out the UI designer!
If Kalzumeus takes off then I can always hire myself a designer later to make it look prettier and more unique.
The downside is that if I failed I would be poor, unemployed, and on the wrong side of the ocean for finding convenient work opportunities.
key_insight: Early-stage software entrepreneurs should minimize upfront costs and launch with 'good enough' solutions—free templates, simple architecture—while keeping a conventional income safety net until the business proves itself.
